The operation requirements for using your jetski are:

Before operating the watercraft, read the Owner’s/Operator’s Manual, the Riding Practice Guide, the Riding Instruction card, and all warning/caution labels. Anyone operating the watercraft should also review these materials.

Know and follow local boating laws and operator age/training requirements. jetski recommends a minimum operator age of 16 years old, and minors should be supervised by adults.

All riders must wear a Coast Guard approved personal flotation device (PFD) that is suitable for personal watercraft use.

Wear protective clothing. All riders must wear a wetsuit bottom or clothing that provides equivalent protection, such as thick, tightly woven, sturdy and snug-fitting apparel like denim. Normal swimwear does not adequately protect against forceful water entry.

<PIC>

Eye protection is recommended to keep wind, water, and glare from the sun out of your eyes. Footwear and gloves are also recommended.

Helmets meeting Snell or DOT standards are required for IJSBA-sanctioned races. For recreational riding, you should decide based on your riding conditions, environment, riding style, ability, traffic congestion, and water surface conditions.

NEVER operate the watercraft after consuming alcohol or taking other drugs.

<PIC>

Always perform the preoperation checks before operating the watercraft.

The operator and passenger should always keep both feet in the footwell when the watercraft is in motion. Do not give a ride to a child whose feet cannot reach the floor of the footwell.

<PIC>

Passenger should firmly hold on to the operator.

Always consult your doctor on whether it is safe for you to ride this watercraft if you are pregnant or in poor health.

Do not attempt to modify this watercraft. Modifications may reduce safety and reliability, and render the watercraft unsafe or illegal for use.

Scan carefully for swimmers and stay away from swimming areas. Maintain a safe distance from other boats or watercraft, watch for ski ropes or fishing lines, obey the Rules of the Road, and check behind you before making a turn.

Always carry a fire extinguisher on board.

<PIC>,['Manual20_19', 'Manual20_20', 'Manual20_21', 'Manual20_22']

Your jetski has these key watercraft characteristics:

1. **Steering depends on jet thrust.**  
Jet thrust turns the watercraft. If you release the throttle completely, only minimum thrust is produced. When traveling above trolling speed, your ability to steer will decrease rapidly without throttle. You may still have some turning ability immediately after releasing the throttle, but once the engine slows down, the watercraft will no longer respond to handlebar input until you open the throttle again or slow to trolling speed.

2. **Practice is important.**  
Practice turning in an open area without obstructions until you become familiar with this handling characteristic.

3. **Clear debris safely.**  
Stop the engine and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch before removing any debris or weeds that may have collected around the jet intake.

<PIC>

4. **It is water-jet propelled and has no neutral.**  
This watercraft is water-jet propelled, and the jet pump is directly connected to the engine. That means the watercraft may move whenever the engine is running. There is no “neutral” position.

5. **Keep clear of intake and nozzle areas.**  
Keep away from the intake grate while the engine is on. Long hair, loose clothing, or PFD straps can become entangled in moving parts, which could result in severe injury or drowning.

6. **Do not place objects in the jet nozzle.**  
Never insert any object into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running. Severe injury or death could result from contact with the rotating parts of the jet pump.

<PIC>,['Manual20_24', 'Manual20_25']

When encountering other vessels while operating your jetski, follow the “Rules of the Road.” Your jetski is legally treated as a powerboat, so you must operate it according to the navigation rules and local regulations for the waterway.

When two vessels meet, one is the “stand-on” vessel and the other is the “give-way” vessel:

Stand-on vessel: maintain your course and speed, except when necessary to avoid an immediate collision.

Give-way vessel: take positive, early, and clear action to stay out of the way. Normally, do not cross in front of the stand-on vessel. Slow down or change direction and pass behind it.

If a collision appears unavoidable, neither vessel has priority. Both vessels must act to avoid the collision.

<PIC>

Main encounter situations:

1. Meeting head-on  
If you are approaching another power-driven vessel head-on and there is a risk of collision, neither vessel has the right-of-way. Both should alter course to avoid the accident. In practice, both vessels should turn to starboard (right) so they pass port-to-port.

<PIC>

2. Crossing  
When two power-driven vessels are crossing and there is risk of collision, the vessel that has the other on its starboard (right) side must keep out of the way. If the other vessel is on your right, you are the give-way vessel. If the other vessel is on your port (left) side, you are normally the stand-on vessel and should maintain course and speed while the other gives way.

<PIC>

3. Overtaking  
If you are overtaking another vessel, you are the give-way vessel and must keep clear until you have fully passed. If another vessel is overtaking you, maintain your course and speed so it can pass safely.

Also remember:
- Keep to the right in narrow channels when safe and practical.
- Fishing vessels using nets, lines, or trawls generally have the right-of-way.
- Sailing vessels should normally be given the right-of-way, except in specific situations such as when they are overtaking.
- Always operate defensively, at a safe speed, and keep a safe distance from other boats, swimmers, and obstacles.
- Check behind you before turning, because other boaters may not expect how quickly a jetski can maneuver.

These are condensed rules from the owner’s manual. You should also follow local boating laws and waterway regulations where you ride.,['Manual20_26', 'Manual20_27', 'Manual20_28']

When using your jetski, here’s what you should know about the Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector:

The QSTS selector is located at the left handlebar grip and is used to adjust the trim angle of the watercraft.

Operating the QSTS selector changes the vertical angle of the jet thrust nozzle, which changes the trim angle of the watercraft.

There are 5 positions total:
- A and B: bow down
- N: neutral
- C and D: bow up

To change the trim angle:
1. Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.
2. Squeeze the shift lock lever, then turn the QSTS selector to the desired position.
3. Release the shift lock lever to lock the selector in place.

<PIC>

CAUTION:
Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating the watercraft at full throttle, otherwise damage could occur to the QSTS.

The neutral “N” position provides good performance for most operating conditions. If needed, you can select bow down or bow up to suit specific riding conditions.

<PIC>

Bow up puts less of the bow in the water. This reduces water resistance, which can improve straight-ahead acceleration while on plane and increase top speed. However, in some conditions the watercraft may begin to “porpoise” (hop in the water). If that happens, return the selector to neutral or choose a bow-down position.

<PIC>,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

On your WaveRunner, these two meters are part of the multifunction information center:

**Fuel meter**
The fuel meter lets you conveniently check the fuel level while riding. It has **eight segments** that show the amount of fuel remaining in the fuel tank. Because the segment display can vary depending on operating conditions, it should be used as a **reference** rather than an exact measurement.

<PIC>

When fuel gets low, the **lowest two segments blink**. According to the manual, blinking indicates the fuel remaining is **less than about 13 L (3.4 US gal, 2.9 Imp gal)**.

**Hour meter**
The hour meter helps you keep track of **engine operating hours** so you can follow the recommended **maintenance schedule**. It shows the total elapsed engine running time since the watercraft was new.

<PIC>

**Note:** The recorded hours are retained even if the battery terminals are disconnected.,['Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

To inspect the fuel filter and fuel tank on your WaveRunner:

1. Fuel filter  
This watercraft is equipped with a one-piece, disposable fuel filter. Inspect the fuel filter and replace it after the initial 10 hours or first month of operation, and every 200 hours or 24 months thereafter, or anytime water is found in the filter.

<PIC>

Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself. An incorrectly installed filter can leak gasoline, which could lead to fire or explosion. If replacement is needed, have a Yamaha dealer perform the service.

2. Fuel tank  
Check the fuel tank for any signs of leakage or for water in the tank.

<PIC>

If water is found in the fuel system, or if the fuel tank needs cleaning, have a Yamaha dealer service the watercraft.,['Manual20_81', 'Manual20_82']

You can adjust the Adjustable Sponsons to change handling based on your riding preference and water conditions.

- Up position: Less water resistance, which improves straight-ahead acceleration when on plane and can improve top speed.
- Down position: Gives the watercraft more “hook,” which improves turning performance.

<PIC>

To adjust them:

1. Remove the bolts on both sponsons.

<PIC>

2. Remove both sponsons, then reinstall them in the desired position.

Note: Make sure both sponsons are installed at the same level.

3. Tighten the bolts on both sponsons to the specified torque.

If weeds or debris get caught in the intake or impeller, cavitation can occur. This can reduce jet thrust even though engine speed increases. If you suspect the jet intake or impeller is clogged, beach the watercraft and inspect those areas. Always stop the engine before beaching the watercraft.

<PIC>

Before removing any weeds or debris from the jet intake or impeller area, shut the engine off and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch. Contact with the rotating parts of the jet pump could cause severe injury or death.

1. Turn the watercraft onto its port (left) side as shown. Place a clean cloth or carpet underneath it to help prevent abrasions and scratches. Support the bow so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

2. Remove any weeds or debris from around the drive shaft, impeller, pump housing, and jet thrust nozzle.

If the debris is difficult to remove, consult your Yamaha dealer.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here’s how to start your WaveRunner in different situations.

First, attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch. The engine will not start if the clip is not installed.

<PIC>

Then push the start switch (green button) to start the engine.

<PIC>

Before starting off, scan constantly for people, swimmers, objects, and other watercraft. Keep in mind that you need throttle to steer, so do not completely release the throttle when trying to avoid something.

<PIC>

Starting and boarding in shallow water:
1. Do not start the engine in less than 60 cm (2 ft) of water. Move the watercraft to water deep enough, then board from the side or rear.
<PIC>
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
3. Grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and start off.

Starting from a dock:
1. Board the watercraft from the side.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock, grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and start off.
<PIC>

Boarding alone:
1. Swim to the rear of the watercraft. Place both hands on the boarding platform, pull yourself up, and grasp the handgrip with one hand.
<PIC>
2. Pull yourself up into a kneeling position on the platform, then move to the seat and sit astride.
<PIC>
3. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
4. Grip the handlebars firmly with both hands, place both feet on the floor of the footwell, start the engine, and look in all directions before starting off.
<PIC>

Boarding with a passenger:
Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated, has both feet on the floor of the footwell, and is holding on to the operator.
1. Climb on board, sit astride the seat, attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.
2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.
3. Have the passenger get on board and sit on the seat.
<PIC>
4. Check that the passenger has both feet on the floor of the footwell and is securely holding on to the operator.
5. Start the engine, look in all directions, and then accelerate to planing speed.
<PIC>,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_87', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_71', 'Manual20_72', 'Manual20_73', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_92']

Basic steps for boarding with a passenger in deep water:

1. Climb on board first as described for boarding alone, sit astride the seat, attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, and install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.

2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.

3. Have the passenger get on board and sit on the seat.  
<PIC>

When the passenger is getting on board, both the passenger and the operator should try to balance the watercraft.

4. The operator should check that the passenger has both feet on the floor of the footwell and is securely holding on to the operator.

5. Start the engine, look in all directions, and then accelerate to planing speed.  
<PIC>

Before starting off, make sure that there are no boats, obstructions, or swimmers around you.  
<PIC>

Important: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated with his or her feet on the floor of the footwell and is holding on to the operator.

Note: Do not operate the watercraft when the total weight of the operator, passenger, and any cargo exceeds 160 kg (353 lb).,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

If it is difficult to balance the watercraft at a standstill, proceed as follows:

1. The passenger should steady the watercraft while the operator boards. The operator should pull himself up onto the boarding platform into a seating position, and then balance the watercraft.
<PIC>

2. Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, then install the clip to the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle.

3. The passenger should then pull himself up onto the platform, kneel down, and crawl onto the seat as the watercraft accelerates.
<PIC>

4. Once both riders are aboard, accelerate the watercraft to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.
<PIC>,['jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

You can identify the phone’s current status by checking how the LED indicator behaves on the handset and the base station.

For the handset LED indicator:

**Events status**
- **On**: Audio boost is on.
- **Off**: The handset is in a call or in standby mode.
- **Flashing**:
  - There is a new message.
  - There is a new voicemail.
  - There is a missed call.
  - The phone is muted.

**Charging status**
- **On**:
  - The handset is on the base or charging cradle in standby mode.
  - Audio boost is on during a call.
- **Off**: The handset is off the base or charging cradle.
- **Flashing**: The phone is muted.

For the base station LED indicator:

<PIC>

**Message status**
- **Steady on**:
  - There is no new message and the memory is not full.
  - The handset is switched on.
  - Paging mode is entered.
- **Flashing**:
  - There is a new message and the memory is not full.
  - There is an incoming call.
  - Recording an incoming message or announcement.
  - Message playback.
  - Remote access or listening to messages from the handset.
- **Flashing fast**:
  - There is no new message and the memory is full.
  - Registration mode is entered.,['Manual22_40', 'Manual22_46']

To load a lawn mower onto a trailer or truck safely:

Use a heavy-duty trailer or truck to transport the machine. Use a full-width ramp, and make sure the trailer or truck has the required brakes, lights, and markings required by law.

<PIC>

Important ramp guidelines:
- Use only a full-width ramp; do not use separate ramps for each side of the machine.
- Do not exceed a 15° angle between the ramp and the ground, or between the ramp and the trailer or truck.
- Make sure the ramp is at least 4 times as long as the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground.

Loading steps:
1. If you are using a trailer, connect it to the tow vehicle and attach the safety chains.
2. If applicable, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
3. Lower the ramp, making sure the ramp angle does not exceed 15°.
4. Back the machine up the ramp.

<PIC>

5. Once the mower is on the trailer, shut off the engine, remove the key, and engage the parking brake.
6. Tie down the machine near the front caster wheels and the rear bumper using straps, chains, cables, or ropes.

<PIC>

Use extreme caution when operating the mower on a ramp. Avoid sudden acceleration or deceleration, as this can cause loss of control or tip-over. When unloading, lower the ramp and drive the machine forward down the ramp.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower:

1. Lower the ramp, making sure it is a full-width ramp and that the angle between the ramp and the ground does not exceed 15 degrees.

<PIC>

2. Use extreme caution on the ramp, and drive the machine forward down the ramp. Avoid sudden acceleration or deceleration, as this could cause loss of control or tip-over.

<PIC>,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

Change the engine oil every 100 hours, or more often in dirty or dusty conditions. Dispose of used oil at a recycling center.

Before you begin, identify whether your mower has a Kawasaki or Kohler engine, since the drain and refill illustrations differ slightly.

1. Start the engine and let it run for 5 minutes.
   This warms the oil so it drains better.
2. Park the machine so that the drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side to help the oil drain completely.
3. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.
5. Drain the oil from the engine.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>

6. Slowly pour approximately 80% of the specified oil into the filler tube, then add the remaining oil gradually until it reaches the Full mark on the dipstick.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>

7. Start the engine and drive the machine to a flat area.
8. Shut off the engine and check the oil level again. Add oil if needed to bring it to the Full mark.

Oil capacity:
• Kawasaki FX751 and FX801: 2.3 L with a filter change; 2.1 L without a filter change.
• Kawasaki FX921: 1.9 L with a filter change; 1.7 L without a filter change.
• Kohler: 1.9 L with a filter change; 1.6 L without a filter change.

Notes:
• Kawasaki oil type: detergent oil, API service SF, SG, SH, SJ, or SL.
• Kohler oil type: detergent oil, API service SJ or higher.
• For additional viscosity guidance, refer to the engine oil specification chart in the manual or the engine owner’s manual.,['Manual23_75', 'Manual23_86', 'Manual23_76', 'Manual23_87']

To remove the air-cleaner filters on your lawn mower, first identify whether your machine has a **Kawasaki** or **Kohler** engine, then follow the matching steps below.

## Before you begin
1. Park the machine on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO).
3. Engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.

## For Kawasaki engines
1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
<PIC>
3. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
4. Gently slide the **primary filter** out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Avoid knocking the filter into the side of the body.
5. Remove the **safety filter** only if you are replacing it.

## For Kohler engines
1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the **air-inlet cover** off the air-cleaner body.
3. Clean the air-inlet screen and cover.
4. Reinstall the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.
<PIC>
5. Release the latches on the air cleaner again.
6. Pull the **air-cleaner cover** off the air-cleaner body.
7. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
8. Gently slide the **primary filter** out of the air-cleaner body.
   - Avoid knocking the filter into the side of the body.
<PIC>
9. Remove the **inner filter** only if you intend to replace it.
   - Never attempt to clean the inner filter.

If you want, I can also provide the **installation steps** for putting the new filters back in.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_82', 'Manual23_83']

To replace the mower belt:

1. Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake.
2. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
3. Lower the mower to the 76 mm (3 inches) height of cut.
4. Remove the belt covers.

<PIC>

5. Insert a 3/8-inch ratchet into the square hole in the idler arm to relieve tension on the idler spring.
6. Remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley.
7. Remove the belt guide on the spring-loaded idler arm.
8. Remove the old belt.
9. Route the new belt around the mower pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine.

<PIC>

Figure 74:
1. Spring  
2. Clutch pulley  
3. Mower belt  
4. Spring-loaded idler assembly  
5. Ratchet  
6. Square hole in the idler arm for the ratchet

10. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
11. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole, reinstall the idler spring. Make sure that both spring ends are seated in the anchor grooves.
12. Reinstall the belt covers.

<PIC>,['Manual23_99', 'Manual23_100', 'Manual23_101']

<PIC>

- **Single VGA/PCIe card:** install it in **PCIe 3.0 x16_1 (gray)**. This slot runs at **x16** and is the recommended slot for best performance.
- **Dual VGA/PCIe cards:** install them in **PCIe 3.0 x16_1** and **PCIe 3.0 x16_2**. They will run at **x8 / x8**.
- **Triple VGA/PCIe cards:** install them in **PCIe 3.0 x16_1**, **PCIe 3.0 x16_2**, and **PCIe 3.0 x16_3**. They will run at **x8 / x8 / x4**.

If you want to adjust the **PCIEx16_1 / PCIEx16_2 link speed** in BIOS, go to:

**Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration > PCIEx16_1 / PCIEx16_2 Link Speed**

Available options:
**[Auto] [Gen1] [Gen2] [Gen3]**

Notes:
- The **slot bandwidth mode** (x16, x8/x8, x8/x8/x4) depends on the number of installed cards.
- For multiple graphics cards, make sure the system has **sufficient power**.
- ASUS also recommends connecting a chassis fan to **CHA_FAN1/2/3** for better cooling when using multiple GPUs.,['Manual25_31']

Before setting any motherboard jumper, turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.

For Clear RTC RAM (2-pin CLRTC):
<PIC>
To erase the RTC RAM:
1. Turn OFF the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Use a metal object such as a screwdriver to short the two pins.
3. Plug the power cord and turn ON the computer.
4. Hold down the <Del> key during the boot process and enter BIOS setup to reenter the data.

If the steps above do not help, remove the onboard battery and short the two pins again to clear the CMOS RTC RAM data. After clearing the CMOS, reinstall the battery.

For CPU Over Voltage jumper (3-pin CPU_OV):
<PIC>
The CPU Over Voltage jumper allows you to set a higher CPU voltage for a flexible overclocking system, depending on the type of the installed CPU.

You can update the motherboard BIOS file using **EZ Flash 3**, **EZ Update**, or **BIOS Updater**.

## 1. Using EZ Flash 3
<PIC>

1. Enter the **Advanced Mode** of the BIOS setup program.
2. Go to **Tool** > **ASUS EZ Flash 3 Utility**, then press **Enter**.
3. Choose how you want to update the BIOS:

### Via USB
1. Insert the USB flash drive that contains the latest BIOS file.
2. Select **by USB**.
3. Press **Tab** to switch to the **Drive** field.
4. Use the **Up/Down** arrow keys to select the USB flash drive, then press **Enter**.
5. Press **Tab** to switch to the **Folder Info** field.
6. Select the BIOS file and press **Enter** to start the BIOS update.

### Via Internet
1. Select **by Internet**.
2. Use the **Left/Right** arrow keys to select the Internet connection method, then press **Enter**.
3. Follow the onscreen instructions to complete the update.

4. Reboot the system after the update is complete.

**Notes:**
- EZ Flash 3 supports USB devices in **FAT32/FAT16** format with a **single partition** only.
- Make sure the Internet connection is working before using the online update method.
- **Do not shut down or reset the system during the BIOS update.**

## 2. Using EZ Update in AI Suite 3
<PIC>

EZ Update allows you to update the motherboard BIOS from within Windows.

1. Open **AI Suite 3** and click **EZ Update** on the main menu bar.
2. To update from the Internet, click **Check Now!** and follow the prompts.
3. To manually update the BIOS, click the browse button (**...**) and select the downloaded BIOS file.
4. If needed, you can also select a boot logo using **MyLogo**.
5. Click **Update** to start updating the BIOS.

**Note:** EZ Update requires an **Internet connection**.

## 3. Using BIOS Updater in DOS
<PIC>

Before updating:
- Prepare the motherboard **support DVD** and a **USB flash drive**.
- Download the latest BIOS file and **BIOS Updater**, then save them to the USB flash drive.
- The USB flash drive must use **FAT32/FAT16** format and a **single partition**.

To update in DOS:
1. Insert the USB flash drive with the BIOS file and BIOS Updater.
2. Boot the system and press **F8** to open the boot device selection screen.
3. Insert the support DVD and boot from the optical drive.
4. When the boot message appears, press **Enter** within 5 seconds to enter **FreeDOS**.
5. At the FreeDOS prompt, type `d:` and press **Enter** to switch to the USB drive.
6. Then type:

`bupdater /pc /g`

7. Press **Enter**.
8. Select the BIOS file and confirm the update.

<PIC>

9. Press **Tab** to switch between the **Drives** panel and **Files** panel if needed.
10. Select the BIOS file and press **Enter**.
11. When prompted, select **Yes** to confirm the BIOS update.

<PIC>

12. After the update is complete, press **Esc** to exit BIOS Updater and restart the computer.

**Important:**
- **Do not shut down or reset the system while updating the BIOS**, or the system may fail to boot.
- After updating, it is recommended to load the BIOS default settings by selecting **Load Optimized Defaults** in the BIOS **Exit** menu.,['Manual25_61', 'Manual25_60', 'Manual25_63', 'Manual25_64']

To create a RAID array on this motherboard, follow these steps:

1. Connect your hard drives to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors on the motherboard. This motherboard supports RAID 0, 1, 5, and 10 through the onboard Intel chipset.

<PIC>

These connectors are set to [AHCI] by default. If you want to create a Serial ATA RAID set, change the SATA Mode item in the BIOS to [RAID].

2. Enter the BIOS, then press <F11> or open the EZ Tuning Wizard from the BIOS screen.

<PIC>

3. In EZ Tuning Wizard, click RAID, then click Yes to enable RAID.
- Make sure your HDDs do not already contain existing RAID volumes.
- Make sure the drives are connected to the Intel SATA connectors.

4. The available HDDs will be displayed. Click Next to continue.

<PIC>

5. Select the storage function for your RAID, then click Next:
- Easy Backup
- Super Speed

<PIC>

6. If you select Easy Backup, choose one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
- Easy Backup (RAID1)
- Easy Backup (RAID10)

<PIC>

You can only select Easy Backup (RAID10) if four (4) HDDs are connected.

7. If you select Super Speed, choose one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
- Super Speed (RAID0)
- Super Speed (RAID5)

<PIC>

8. After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue the RAID setup.

9. When the RAID setup is complete, click Yes to exit, then click OK to reset the system.

After rebooting, the RAID volume will be created based on the mode you selected.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_71', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

Before installing the CPU, take these precautions:

<PIC>

- Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.
- Before handling components, use a grounded wrist strap or touch a safely grounded object or a metal object, such as the power supply case, to avoid damage from static electricity.
- Hold components by the edges to avoid touching the ICs on them.
- If you remove any component, place it on a grounded antistatic pad or in the bag that came with the component.
- Before you install or remove any component, ensure that the ATX power supply is switched off or the power cord is detached from the power supply.

This motherboard comes with a surface mount LGA1151 socket designed for 6th Generation processors.

<PIC>

CPU socket LGA1151

Unplug all power cables before installing the CPU.

- Make sure you install only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket. Do not install a CPU designed for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets.
- When you receive the motherboard, check that the PnP cap is installed on the socket and that the socket contacts are not bent.
- If the PnP cap is missing, or if you find any damage to the cap, socket contacts, or motherboard components, contact your retailer immediately.
- Keep the PnP cap after installation, as it is required for Return Merchandise Authorization (RMA).
- Damage to socket contacts caused by incorrect CPU installation or removal, or by loss or incorrect removal of the PnP cap, is not covered by the warranty.,['Manual25_5', 'Manual25_9']

To know the system memory information of this motherboard, you can check it in several ways:

1. Check the motherboard specifications. This motherboard supports **4 x DDR4 DIMM slots**, **up to 64 GB** total memory, and memory frequencies from **2133 MHz** up to **3400 MHz (O.C.)**. It also supports **dual-channel memory architecture** and **XMP**.

<PIC>

2. Check the physical DIMM slots on the motherboard. The four memory slots are labeled **DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2**. For better compatibility, the manual recommends:
   - **1 DIMM:** install in **A2**
   - **2 DIMMs:** install in **A2 and B2**
   - **4 DIMMs:** install in **A1, A2, B1, and B2**

<PIC>

3. Check the installed memory in BIOS. Enter BIOS **Advanced Mode** and open the **Main** menu. This screen shows basic memory information such as **Total Memory** and **Memory Frequency**.

<PIC>

4. For more detailed memory module information, go to **Tool > SPD Information** in BIOS. There you can view the **SPD (Serial Presence Detect)** data for each installed DIMM by selecting the slot: **DIMM_A1, DIMM_B1, DIMM_A2, or DIMM_B2**.

If you want, I can also help you identify whether your RAM is installed in the best slots for dual-channel mode.,['Manual25_2', 'Manual25_18', 'Manual25_78']

The **Serial port connector (10-1 pin COM)** on the motherboard is an **internal header** used to add a **serial (COM) port** to the system.

You need to connect a **serial port module cable** to this header, and then mount the module to an opening at the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

A few key points:
- This is a **10-1 pin COM header** on the motherboard, not a rear I/O serial port by itself.
- The **COM module is purchased separately**.
- The diagram shows both the **header location on the motherboard** and the **pin definition** for the COM connector, including **PIN 1**.

In BIOS, you can configure it under **Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration**:
- **Serial Port [On]**: enable or disable the serial port (COM)
- **Change Settings**: select the Serial Port base address, such as:
  - **IO=3F8h; IRQ=4**
  - **IO=2F8h; IRQ=3**
  - **IO=3E8h; IRQ=4**
  - **IO=2E8h; IRQ=3**,['Manual25_39']

The TPM connector (14-1 pin TPM) is an internal motherboard header for connecting a Trusted Platform Module (TPM). It supports a TPM system that securely stores keys, digital certificates, passwords, and data. A TPM system also helps enhance network security, protect digital identities, and ensure platform integrity.

<PIC>

The image above shows the TPM connector location on the motherboard and the 14-1 pin pinout for reference.,['Manual25_40']

The **Thermal sensor connector (2-pin T_SENSOR)** is a header on the motherboard used for a **thermistor cable**. After connecting the thermistor cable, it lets you monitor the temperature of a specific area, such as a critical motherboard component or a connected device.

<PIC>

**Pin definition:**
- **Pin 1:** SENSOR IN
- **Pin 2:** GND

**Note:** The **thermistor cable is purchased separately**.

After the sensor is connected, you can check its reading in **BIOS > Monitor** as **T_Sensor Temperature**. If no thermistor cable is connected, this item may show **N/A**. It can also be selected as a **temperature source for chassis fan or extension fan Q-Fan control**.

<PIC>,['Manual25_43', 'Manual25_83']

The anti-block shield is a safety part on the pressure cooking lid. It helps prevent food particles from entering the steam release pipe, which supports proper pressure regulation during pressure cooking. It is required for safe pressure cooking operation.

<PIC>

Before using the pressure cooking lid, check the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve to make sure they are not clogged.

To remove the anti-block shield: hold the lid like a steering wheel, then use your thumbs to press firmly against the side of the anti-block shield, pushing toward the side of the lid and upward until it pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

To install the anti-block shield: position it over the prongs and press down until it snaps into place. Do not use the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed.

The steam release valve is controlled by the quick release button — this determines whether the cooker is sealed or releasing pressure.

To set it:

Although the lid says **Turn to Seal**, this is more of a light **flicking motion**. Do **not** turn the quick release button more than **45°**; let it spring back to its original position.

2. **Vent position:** press the quick release button **down** until it **clicks and locks** into the **Vent** position.

To install the anti-block shield on the pressure cooking lid, place the anti-block shield over the prongs on the underside of the lid, then press down firmly until it snaps into position.

Hold one finger on the flat top of the float valve, then turn the lid over. Firmly attach the silicone cap to the bottom of the float valve.

The condensation collector sits at the back of the cooker base and catches any overflow from the condensation rim. It should be installed before cooking, then emptied and rinsed after each use.

To install the condensation collector:
1. Locate the tabs on the back of the cooker base.
2. Align the grooves on the condensation collector with the tabs.
3. Slide the condensation collector into place until it is fitted securely.

<PIC>,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring:

Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack and press it firmly into place all the way around. Press down well to prevent puckering or uneven fit.

When installed properly, the sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

CAUTION: Only use authorized Multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer sealing rings. DO NOT use a stretched or damaged sealing ring.,['Manual30_31']

Always unplug your cooker and let it cool to room temperature before cleaning.

To remove the silicone sealing ring:
1. Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring.
2. Pull the sealing ring out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

After removing the sealing ring, inspect the steel rack to make sure it is secure, centered, and at an even height all the way around the lid. Do not attempt to repair a deformed sealing ring rack.

Tip: The sealing ring is easiest to remove and install when it is wet and soapy.

<PIC>,['Manual30_30']

The sealing ring is part of the pressure cooking lid. When the pressure cooking lid is closed, the sealing ring creates an air-tight seal between the lid and the inner pot.

The sealing ring must be installed before using the cooker, and only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

Tip: Silicone is porous, so it can absorb strong aromas and certain flavors. Keeping extra sealing rings on hand can help reduce odor and flavor transfer between dishes.

<PIC>

CAUTION  
Only use authorized Multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer sealing rings. DO NOT use a stretched or damaged sealing ring.

Always check the sealing ring for cuts, deformation, and correct installation before cooking. With normal use, sealing rings can stretch over time and should be replaced every 12-18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, or damage.

Failure to follow these instructions may cause food to discharge, which may lead to personal injury or property damage.

To remove the sealing ring: grip the edge of the silicone and pull the sealing ring out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

<PIC>

To install the sealing ring: place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack and press it into place. Press down firmly to prevent puckering. When installed properly, the sealing ring should fit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve sits loosely on the steam release pipe on the pressure cooking lid. When the cooker releases pressure, steam ejects from the top of the steam release valve. This part is essential for product safety and is required for pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Warning: Do not cover or block the steam release valve in any way. Steam released from the valve is extremely hot, so keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from it during pressure release.

The quick release button controls the steam release valve, meaning it determines whether the cooker is in Seal or Vent mode. In the Seal position, the button is up. To release pressure, press the quick release button down until it locks into the Vent position; a jet of steam will then come out through the top of the steam release valve.

<PIC>

For care and cleaning:
Remove the steam release valve by pulling it straight up and off the steam release pipe. The steam release valve must be installed before using the cooker.

<PIC>

To install it, place the steam release valve onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When installed correctly, it will still sit loosely on the pipe, but it should stay in place even if the lid is turned over.

<PIC>,['Manual30_15', 'Manual30_14', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button controls the steam release valve — the part that determines whether the cooker stays sealed or releases pressure.

<PIC>

Here’s what to know:
- **Seal position:** the button is up. The lid stays sealed for pressure cooking.
- **Vent position:** press the button down to release pressure.

What we love about it: It’s easy to use and helps keep your hands farther away from the steam.

What you should know about it: Although the lid says **Turn to Seal**, it works more like a quick flick. Do **not** turn the quick release button more than **45°**, and let it spring back to its original position.

For **Quick Release**, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the **Vent** position.

<PIC>

When it is pressed down, a jet of steam will come out from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. When all pressure has been released, the **float valve drops down into the lid**.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Do **not** place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve while releasing pressure.
- Do **not** try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped.
- Do **not** use Quick Release for **fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods**, or foods that expand during cooking.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release (NR or NPR) is a pressure-release method used after pressure cooking.

With Natural Release, the cooker is not vented manually. Instead, as the temperature inside the cooker drops, the pressure decreases naturally over time, so cooking stops gradually.

<PIC>

What to do:
- After cooking is complete, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position.
- Turn Keep Warm off.

What to expect:
- The food will continue to cook while the cooker depressurizes. This is sometimes called carryover cooking or resting.
- Depressurization time depends on the amount, type, and temperature of the food and liquid inside, and it can take up to 40 minutes.
- When pressure has been fully released, the float valve drops into the lid.

NOTICE:
Use Natural Release for high-starch foods such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, and congee, as well as foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains.

Important:
Do not try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped completely.,['Manual30_9']

The two primary modes are:

**Virtual Wall Mode:** When the switch is in the **“up”** position, the device works as a virtual wall. It creates an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can see and can block openings of up to **10 ft (3 m)**.

**Halo Mode:** When the switch is in the **“down”** position, the device creates a protective zone that the vacuum will not enter. This is useful for protecting items such as a **dog bowl or vase**, or for keeping the vacuum out of small undesired areas. The halo reaches about **24 inches from the center** of the device.

To empty the vacuum cleaner’s bin, follow these steps:

This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls or vases, or keeping the vacuum away from small areas like corners or under desks. The halo reaches about **24 inches from the center** of the device.

<PIC>

**Virtual Wall Mode:** When the switch is in the **up** position, it creates an invisible barrier in a line-like cone shape to block off openings or passageways. It can block an area up to **10 feet (3 m)**.

<PIC>

To start the engine of a snowmobile, follow these steps carefully:

Before starting the engine:
- Read the safety information carefully.
- Apply the parking brake.
- Make sure the engine stop switch is in the “ON” position.

<PIC>

1. Fully open the starter lever (choke).
- ① Starter lever (choke)
- ② Fully open: for cold engine starting
- ③ Half-open: for warming up the engine (CS340E/ET410TR/VK540E)
- ④ Close: for warm engine starting

For manual starting models:

2. Turn the main switch to the “ON” position.
- ① “ON”

3. Pull the recoil starter slowly until it engages, then pull it briskly. After the engine starts, move the starter lever (choke) to the “Half-open” position. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

<PIC>

For electric starting models:

2. Turn the main switch to the “START” position.
- ① “START”

<PIC>

After the engine starts, move the starter lever (choke) to the “Half-open” position. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

Caution:
- Release the switch immediately after the engine starts.
- If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and try again.
- Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to preserve the battery.
- Do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds in one attempt.,['Manual34_111', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

The proper way to use the steering system on a snowmobile is:

Before riding, check the throttle, brake, and steering to make sure they operate properly before starting the engine.

<PIC>

When riding, always keep both hands on the handlebars. To steer, turn the handlebar in the direction you want to go.

For most snow surfaces, turning also requires “body English.” As you approach a curve, slow down and begin turning the handlebars in the desired direction. At the same time, put your weight on the running board on the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn.

<PIC>

Practice this turning technique many times at low speed in a large, flat area with no obstacles. Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

To turn a snowmobile correctly on most snow surfaces, use proper body positioning along with steering.

1. As you approach a curve, slow down.
2. Begin turning the handlebars in the direction you want to go.
3. At the same time, shift your weight to the inside running board.
4. Lean your upper body into the turn.
5. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you should lean.

It is best to practice this at low speed in a large, flat area with no obstacles until you are comfortable with the technique.

Avoid abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movement, or entering a turn too fast, as these can cause the snowmobile to tip.

If the snowmobile begins to tip during a turn, lean farther into the turn to help regain balance. If needed, gradually reduce the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn.

<PIC>

Remember: Avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with the operation of your snowmobile.,['Manual34_127']

Proper techniques for riding uphill on a snowmobile:

- Start by practicing on gentle slopes. Attempt steeper or more difficult climbs only after you have developed your riding skills.
- As you approach a hill, accelerate before beginning the climb, then reduce the throttle opening to help prevent track slippage.
- Keep your weight on the uphill side at all times. When climbing straight up a hill, lean forward. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars.

<PIC>

- Slow down as you reach the crest of the hill, and be prepared for obstacles, sharp drop-offs, or other people or vehicles on the other side.
- If you cannot continue up the hill, do not spin the track. Stop the engine and set the parking brake. Then pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back downhill. Do not get on the downhill side of the machine. Once it is pointed downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend the hill.

Warning: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobilers.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill, keep your speed to a minimum. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged while descending. This helps the snowmobile use engine compression to slow down and prevents it from rolling freely down the hill. Also, apply the brake frequently using light pressure.

<PIC>

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking during descent. Excessive braking can lock the track and cause a loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Side hills are not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobilers.

When crossing a slope, proper body position is essential to maintain balance:
- Lean your body toward the uphill side.
- A recommended position is to kneel with the knee of your downhill leg on the seat and keep the foot of your uphill leg on the running board.
- This makes it easier to shift your weight as needed.

<PIC>

Because snow and ice are slippery, your snowmobile may begin to slide sideways. If that happens:
- Steer in the direction of the slide, as long as there are no obstacles in your path.
- Once you regain balance, gradually steer back in the direction you want to travel.

If the snowmobile starts to tip, steer downhill to help regain balance.

If you cannot maintain balance and the snowmobile is going to tip over, dismount immediately on the uphill side.,['Manual34_129']

Steps to inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile:

1. Let the engine cool first, then periodically remove and inspect the spark plug. Heat and deposits will cause any spark plug to slowly break down and erode over time.

2. Check the color of the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. The ideal color is medium to light tan for a normally ridden machine. If the spark plug shows a distinctly different color, there may be an engine problem. In that case, have the machine checked by your dealer rather than trying to diagnose it yourself.

3. Verify that the spark plug has the correct thread length (reach) for your model. If the reach is too long, overheating and engine damage may result. If the reach is too short, spark plug fouling and poor performance may result.

<PIC>

BR250T: 12.7 mm (0.50 in)
CS340E / ET410TR / VK540E: 19.0 mm (0.75 in)

4. Before installing the spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust it to specification.

<PIC>

5. When reinstalling the plug, always clean the gasket surface, wipe any grime from the threads, and tighten the spark plug properly.

Spark plug torque: 28 Nm (2.8 m-kg, 20 ft-lb),['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

Here are the steps to clean a snowmobile:

1. Thoroughly clean the machine, inside and out, to remove any corrosive salts and acids that may have built up.
2. Use Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent product, to loosen mud, grease, and grime.
3. Wash the snowmobile with mild soap.
4. Rinse it completely.
5. Dry it completely.

If the snowmobile was transported through areas where road salt is used, be sure to clean it as soon as you reach your destination to remove any corrosive salt.

Common causes and conditions that can lead to poor television or radio reception include:

1. IGNITION INTERFERENCE: The picture may flutter, drift, or show black spots and horizontal streaking. This is usually caused by interference from automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, and other electrical appliances.
<PIC>

2. GHOSTS: Ghosting happens when the TV signal travels along two paths instead of one. One path is direct, and the other is reflected by tall buildings, hills, or other large objects. Changing the direction or position of the antenna may improve reception.
<PIC>

3. SNOW: If the receiver is in a weak or fringe signal area, the picture may be covered with small dots or noise. Installing a special or better antenna may help improve reception.
<PIC>

Other possible causes include:
- Strong, random signals from a car or airplane interfering with the TV signal.
- A weak signal from the antenna.
- Severely weathered antennas or antenna cables reducing signal quality.
- Local interference.
- Poor antenna connections.

For best reception, checking antenna connections, adjusting the antenna, and using AUTO PROGRAM again if signal quality is poor may help.,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03']

Closed captioning converts the audio portion of a TV program into written words on the screen, similar to subtitles. Text modes can also show full-screen information services, such as news or weather, if the station provides them.

<PIC>

To enable or change captions/text, you can use either of these methods:

Using the CAPTION button:
1. Press the CAPTION button on the remote.
2. Press the CAPTION button repeatedly to choose one of the available options: OFF, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
3. Press the ENTER button to exit.

<PIC>

Using the MENU button:
1. Press the MENU button.
2. Use the up/down buttons to select the Special menu.
3. Press the right button, then use the up/down buttons to select Caption.
4. Press the right button again, then use the up/down buttons to select Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
5. Repeatedly press the MENU button to exit.

<PIC>

Notes:
· Mode1 is usually the normal closed-caption signal.
· Mode2 may carry alternate caption information, such as program-related or demonstration information.
· Text1 and Text2 are text services that may display full-screen information such as weather, news, or program listings, if broadcast by the station.
· Not all channels or programs provide caption or text signals.
· The TV remembers the last caption/text mode selected, even after the TV is turned off.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

To properly connect and set up an outdoor antenna for the best signal reception, follow these steps:

For the best reception, use an outdoor antenna. Before connecting it, inspect the antenna and antenna cables, because severely weathered antennas and cables can reduce signal quality.

3. If the picture shows ghosting or looks weak/snowy, adjust the direction or position of the antenna. Changing the antenna direction or position may improve reception.

<PIC>

4. If the picture is marred by small dots in a weak signal area, it may be necessary to install a special antenna to improve the picture.

To properly connect a DVD player for the best results:

1. Connect the DVD player’s component video outputs to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i):
   - DVD Y → TV Y
   - DVD Pb / PB / Cb / B-Y → TV PB
   - DVD Pr / PR / Cr / R-Y → TV PR

2. Connect the DVD player’s stereo audio outputs to the TV’s audio inputs:
   - DVD L audio → TV AUDIO L
   - DVD R audio → TV AUDIO R

<PIC>

3. If the DVD player uses different names for the component video terminals, match them like this:
   - Y → Y
   - Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB → PB
   - Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR → PR

<PIC>

4. Turn on the TV, then press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control or the TV/VIDEO button on the TV front panel to select COMPONENT.

5. Turn on the DVD player, then start playback.

For proper operation, make sure the video cables are connected only to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i) jacks and the audio cables are connected to the matching L/R audio input jacks.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']
